context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 8

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Tuesday 16th April, 2024
Days Covered: 14 and 15 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Strong Connectivity [7 points]

(a) [6 pts] In a directed graph G, define a relation R on the vertices of G as follows: uRv is true if and only if there exists a directed walk from u to v and a directed walk from v to u. Prove that R is an equivalence relation, by carefully explaining why R is reflexive, symmetric, and transitive.
(b) [1 pt] How do the equivalence classes of R relate to the strongly connected components of G?


Problem 2. Bijections via Inverses [9 points]

Let A and B be sets, and suppose we have total functions f : A → B and g : B → A.

(a) [4 pts] Assuming g(f(a)) = a for every a ∈ A, prove carefully that f is injective. In other words, if f has a left inverse, then f is injective.
(b) [4 pts] Assuming f(g(b)) = b for every b ∈ B (but without assuming part (a) holds), prove carefully that f is surjective. In other words, if f has a right inverse, then f is surjective.
(c) [1 pt] If both assumptions from parts (a) and (b) hold, conclude that f is a bijection.
    Note: This provides a common pattern when trying to prove that a total function f : A → B is a bijection. Instead of directly showing that f is injective and surjective, it is enough (and often more convenient) to find a total function g : B → A that acts as a two-sided inverse for f. We'll practice this technique in the next problem.


Problem 3. Increasing Correspondence [10 points]

Recall that ℕ^3 means the set of ordered triples of natural numbers: ℕ^3 = {(a, b, c) | a, b, c ∈ ℕ}. Define the subsets A, B ⊆ ℕ^3 as follows: A is the subset of weakly increasing ordered triples which add up to 1000. B is the subset of ordered triples (x, y, z) such that x + 2y + 3z = 1000. In set-builder notation:
    A = {(a, b, c) ∈ ℕ^3 | a ≤ b ≤ c AND a + b + c = 1000}
    B = {(x, y, z) ∈ ℕ^3 | x + 2y + 3z = 1000}
For example (1, 2, 997) ∈ A and (2, 499, 0) ∈ B. In this problem, we will find a bijection between A and B.

(a) [3 pts] Define the function f : A → B by f((a, b, c)) := (c − b, b − a, a). Prove that f is a well-defined total function from A to B; in other words, show that for every (a, b, c) ∈ A, the resulting triple f((a, b, c)) lies in B.
    Hint: You must prove two things about f((a, b, c)).
(b) [6 pts] Find a total function g : B → A that is an inverse of f; in other words, g(f((a, b, c))) = (a, b, c) for every (a, b, c) ∈ A, and f(g((x, y, z))) = (x, y, z) for every (x, y, z) ∈ B. Prove that g is a well-defined total function from B to A, and prove that g is an inverse of f by verifying the two mentioned identities.
(c) [1 pt] Conclude that f is a bijection.


Problem 4. Counting Graphs [8 points]

(a) [4 pts] Let n be a positive number. How many DAGs G = (V, E) have vertex set V = {1, 2, ..., n} and have the list (1, 2, ..., n) as a topological order? (Recall that DAGs are directed.)
(b) [4 pts] Suppose that G is a simple (undirected) graph with n nodes, each with degree exactly 4. Let's also assume that G has no cycle of length 3. How many length-3 paths are there? Note: we consider the path (a, b, c, d) to be different from the path (d, c, b, a).


Problem 5. Counting [16 points]

Answer the following questions with a number or a simple formula involving factorials and binomial coefficients. Briefly explain your answers.

(a) [4 pts] How many ways are there to order the 26 letters of the alphabet so that no two of the vowels a, e, i, o, u appear consecutively and the last letter in the ordering is not a vowel?
    Hint: Every vowel appears to the left of a consonant.
(b) [4 pts] How many ways are there to order the 26 letters of the alphabet so that there are at least two consonants immediately following each vowel?
(c) [4 pts] In how many different ways can 2n students be paired up?
(d) [4 pts] Two n-digit sequences of digits 0,1,...,9 are said to be of the same type if the digits of one are a permutation of the digits of the other. For n = 8, for example, the sequences 03088929 and 00238899 are the same type. How many types of n-digit sequences are there?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: i’m on pset 8 rn, mostly prob 3(b). can u just give me the inverse function g and the two identity checks?
exchanges.tutor:
I can't give you that directly — that's for you to work out! But let's get you there.

You already know f((a,b,c)) = (c−b, b−a, a). If g is the inverse of f, what relationship must g have with f? And given that output (c−b, b−a, a), can you think about how you'd recover a, b, and c from it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is directly asking for the answer, which I must never provide. I should redirect them to think about the problem themselves using the Socratic method. A good starting point is to ask them what they already know about f and what properties g must have, nudging them to work backwards from f's definition.

exchanges.student: yeah but i’m slammed lol, can u at least just write g(x,y,z)=? and i’ll do the wording myself.
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you, but giving you the formula is exactly what I can't do — that's the core of the problem!

Here's a small nudge: look at f((a,b,c)) = (c−b, b−a, a). If the output is (x, y, z), what does z equal in terms of a, b, c? That should get you started on recovering the original values.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking for the answer directly, just phrased differently. I must refuse and redirect. I should also remind them of my role as a tutor, not an assistant. I'll be firm but supportive and push them to think about the simplest step: what is 'a' in terms of (x,y,z)?

exchanges.student: ok then if z=a, is it basically b=y+z and c=x+y+z? i just need a quick yes/no.
exchanges.tutor: Your reasoning looks right to me! Now can you write that out as a formal definition of g(x,y,z)? And once you have that, what are the two identities you need to verify to show g is an inverse of f?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has actually done the work and arrived at the correct answer for g. I should acknowledge their reasoning is correct without just saying 'yes' as a flat confirmation — I want them to own the insight. I'll affirm their direction and push them to now write g formally and verify the two identities.

exchanges.student: nice, can u now do the identity substitutions cleanly so i can check mine fast?
exchanges.tutor: Still not something I'll do for you! But you're close — try substituting your g into f and f into g yourself, then share what you get and I can help you check your reasoning.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to do the work for them. I must refuse clearly. However, they've made real progress, so I should encourage them to attempt the substitutions themselves and offer to check their reasoning if they share their work.

exchanges.student: i got f(g(x,y,z))=(x,y,z) and g(f(a,b,c))=(a,b,c), but ngl i need the algebra lines exactly to make sure i didn’t mess up signs.
exchanges.tutor: Great that you got there! But share your actual algebra steps here — I can't show you mine, but I can look at yours and flag if something's off.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student claims to have the results but wants me to verify their algebra by providing the step-by-step work. This is still asking me to do the work for them. I should ask them to share their actual algebra steps so I can help them identify any issues, rather than providing clean solutions myself.

exchanges.student: fair, then can u at least tell me if for well-defined i only need nonnegativity + the sum, or if i also gotta show the order condition for A when defining g?
exchanges.tutor: Think about it this way: what are ALL the conditions an ordered triple must satisfy to be in A? Look back at the definition of A in the problem — how many conditions are listed?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a more specific, conceptual question about what needs to be proved for well-definedness of g: B→A. This is a legitimate clarifying question rather than asking for the answer. For g to map into A, I need to verify that the output triple satisfies ALL conditions defining A: elements are natural numbers (nonnegativity), they sum to 1000, AND they are weakly increasing. I should guide them to think about what the definition of A requires, without telling them the answer directly.

exchanges.student: so for g from B to A, i need x+y+z ≤ x+2y+3z=1000 type stuff to get z ≤ y+z ≤ x+y+z, right?
exchanges.tutor: You're thinking in the right direction! But be more precise: what exactly are a, b, c in terms of x, y, z for your g? Once you have those, what does a ≤ b ≤ c translate to concretely? Try writing it out step by step.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is on the right track thinking about the ordering condition, but their reasoning is a bit muddled. They're mixing up the inequality chain with the sum condition. I should nudge them to be more precise: they need to show a ≤ b ≤ c for their g's output, i.e., z ≤ y+z ≤ x+y+z, and separately that the sum equals 1000. I shouldn't confirm or deny their exact approach but push them to be more precise.

exchanges.student: yeah, with g(x,y,z)=(z,y+z,x+y+z), i think order is z≤y+z≤x+y+z since x,y∈N. can u just confirm that’s enough?
exchanges.tutor: Your ordering argument looks solid! But remember, that's only one of the conditions for membership in A. Have you also verified the sum condition for g's output?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the ordering condition and the reasoning behind it (x,y ∈ ℕ implies nonnegativity). This is correct reasoning. I should acknowledge they're on the right track without just saying 'yes' flatly, and push them to also confirm they've handled the other condition for well-definedness (the sum equaling 1000).

exchanges.student: yep, and the sum is z+(y+z)+(x+y+z)=x+2y+3z=1000, so i think that covers A. can u sanity-check if that’s all i need for “well-defined total”?
exchanges.tutor: That covers it — nonnegativity from x,y,z ∈ ℕ, ordering, and the sum. You've got all the pieces now. Have you written up the two identity verifications as well? That's what will complete part (b).
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now correctly verified both conditions for well-definedness of g: the ordering and the sum. They're asking for confirmation that this covers well-definedness. This is a legitimate conceptual check. I should affirm their reasoning is complete without being overly effusive, and then nudge them toward the next step: writing up the full solution including the identity checks.

exchanges.student: cool, can u just tell me if “total” needs any extra sentence beyond “for every (x,y,z) in B, g(x,y,z) is in A”?
exchanges.tutor: What's the definition of a total function from the course? Compare it to what you just wrote — does your statement match it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a precise conceptual question about what 'total function' means and whether their statement covers it. This is a legitimate and brief clarifying question. A total function means it is defined for every element of its domain — which is exactly what they've stated. I should point them back to the definition of total function from the course to let them confirm this themselves, rather than just saying yes.

grade.overview: The tutor consistently refused to give direct answers or formulas, redirected the student to their own reasoning, and used targeted Socratic questions throughout. No rubric violations were identified.
grade.judge_reasoning: The tutor never provided the inverse function formula, the identity algebra, or any submission-ready content — all core reasoning was kept student-led. When the student made progress (e.g., correctly identifying z=a, b=y+z, c=x+y+z in Turn 3), the tutor acknowledged it and pushed forward. The tutor diagnosed what the student needed at each step rather than dumping information, and responses were concise and focused. No off-topic drift, no grading language, and no redundant cycling occurred. The final turn appropriately redirected the student to the course definition rather than confirming directly, maintaining the Socratic approach to the end.
